speaker: interviewer
text: April, 1996. What did you do in the-- in the seven months that you lived in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Waiting to go further.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you occupy yourselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was a lot of occupation. First of all, there you are free-- reasonably free-- and you've got young people around you, boys and girls. And there was always other people who came to join us. Actually, at one time there was 200 Polish, beautiful, young girls came from Sweden, who they-- after-- after the war, from Belsen, when they have been liberated, they were all taken to-- to recuperation to Sweden. I mean, they all sort of recuperated. They all came to us. And it's-- it's always something. And we always sort of-- we read. We kept our mind quite occupied. We-- we played games, a bit of theater, a bit of singing. And there's so many activities young people can do-- playing sports, football. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you didn't work?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, we couldn't work. Actually, the only work we have done in Belgium was that when the American army was pulling out from Belgium, so they needed some-- some help and drivers who was-- was shipping the goods from-- from-- from the-- from the land onto the Antwerp-- Antwerp harbor, and then load them on the ship. And they needed some laborer, and some of us did work. So that give us quite a lot of opportunity to make some money, which we all shared. I mean, there was never any-- in our group of 20, it was all shared. I mean, you can get money from home or you can find money-- it was unquestionable. It was ours. So-- and we had a lot of opportunity to-- to steal, and being on a black market, because there was quite a valuable equipment there we taking-- you know, we've been taking. And the Belgian people were lining up. I mean, there was no real control of it. And there were accumulators, and batteries, and all sorts-- and they paid for it, so we kept the money.

speaker: interviewer
text: And besides that money, who provided you with food?

speaker: interviewee
text: The food was always provided by-- by Joint. They-- they looked after us.

speaker: interviewer
text: On a regular basis, every day?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, you see, we cooked ourselves. I mean, we had our own facilities, but there was never any shortage of food. As a matter of fact, sometimes was-- they send us too much, and you got sick of it, and you really abused the food, which you shouldn't.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the name of the place that you were staying in, in Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was a very quaint, lovely place. It was--

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember the name?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was Chateau [INAUDIBLE]. It was in the vicinity of Liege. And we did travel from there, I mean-- yes, but we-- we always have sort of a-- different identities. It is actually my name in Belgium was [? Lachman ?] Moses. So they had certain legal and valid documents, and that went through times, and times and times so--

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you have documents to say you were [? Lachman ?] Moses?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'm Lachman Moses, yes. And we had to be sort of-- be very careful. And when you get a letter-- like, I got letter from my mother at home, she addressed it to [? Lachman ?] Moses. So you know, the first letter I wrote to Mother I said my name was [? Lachman ?] Moses-- I said-- is your name not good enough? [LAUGHS] Yes, but I explained it all after. [? This OK. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, and where did you go from-- from Belgium?

speaker: interviewee
text: From Belgium, we went to France.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long were you there?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, but to go to France, it was the final sort of a destination. Not to stay in France, but-- France-- but to-- to-- to embark on the ship in France. We didn't know yet how-- and I think there must have been going a lot of cat and mouse game with the British, because there was about 70 trucks that would take us around everywhere. One place-- we didn't know what's happening to us. You know, in circles-- a journey which should have taken maybe five hours took 10 hours, 15 hours. I think that-- I think that was sort of a tactic to disguise something from the-- from the British.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just the same 20 of you, or were there more?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, we were all together, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes, but just the 20?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no, there was 70 trucks of people.

speaker: interviewer
text: Full?

speaker: interviewee
text: Full. You see, there was-- because there we had a boat waiting for us. But the question, where and how are we going to-- to put on those boats, right? So, you know, they come from all over. And-- and I know that two days we've been sitting in the trucks going around, I mean, you know it's wondering what was at the end of it. We-- so we ended up in-- on the border of Spain and France. There was a small harbor-- more of fishing harbor than anything else-- [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewee
text: So, as we came close it, you know, there was about three boats. One was a fairly-- not so big-- fairly large, and the two small boats. And we said, if anything, they're dead boats. [? OK. ?] But once we arrived, I mean, there was a smallest boat. We started to pick us on it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Actually, I think it was about 600 ton or something, and 2,700 people. I mean, we've been loaded on that and without any-- any thought, you know, that-- about what might happen to us. You know, I mean, it was-- we really were being like sheep. You know, because-- not only because of this, but we wanted to go ahead. We wanted to-- we were so keen to get to Israel, you know, to be there.

speaker: interviewee
text: So on the end, we got onto the-- we got onto that-- to that boat. And there was shelves was made. So the-- that's where you can stay. That-- say, if you want to lie down, you went first with your feet in the other one, and then your head, you crawl up there. You could hardly-- if you were a bit larger, you couldn't hardly turn. So, I mean, it was terrible conditions.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was quite a lot of room up on the top, so-- and I realized something. That everyone in the harbor, all the-- the officials, they were all dead drunk. So they make them dead drunk for us to enable to--

speaker: interviewer
text: To-- to board the boat.

speaker: interviewee
text: To board, and to leave. I mean, it's-- So as we really went out, we had certain equipment, you know, lifesaving equipment on the boat-- big, you know, thing. After about two hours outside, we throw everything into the sea. We needed room to move. Most of the people could not stay down in this, so you-- it's just terrible.

speaker: interviewee
text: And looking at the crew, I mean, there was one Israeli from Haganah who was in charge as a captain of the boat, but the crew was all throat-cut people. I mean, if I meet them at night I would get a shock, you know? I mean, who would be-- who would be working on a boat which is illegal, and such a boat which could possibly sink any moment, and captured by British, and so on? So really, the lowest-- the lowest crew which they can get.

speaker: interviewee
text: But a trip which it would have taken, in normal circumstances, a day or six hours, took us seven days. So we were--

speaker: interviewee
text: The trip from-- from--

speaker: interviewee
text: From [INAUDIBLE] to-- to Israel, to the beaches of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Haifa.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were prepared emotionally, and mentally, and physically through this encounter with the British. So we said that we-- we just want, if you should take catch us, we won't give in. And sort of a fighting should go on in such a manner that they should not be able to kill us. So there's no-- we didn't have any-- any weapon or nothing. We just had sticks. And-- and-- but we won't need it anyhow, because we will just ram the shores and came off the boat. We-- listen, to be there, the only thing which you kept going is your optimism, no logic. [LAUGHS] So--

speaker: interviewer
text: But you had some of-- you had enough of that, so that's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: So anyhow, we went-- you know, it was sometimes breaking down, and so we noticed that some [INAUDIBLE] airplane is flying around-- above us. We knew that they are British, so we had to hide ourselves, which meant we couldn't really very much. And at night, you know, they were sending sort of a light Morse sign, you know, asking who we are, what we are, where we're going. And we told them that we are a librarian sort of boat, and we're taking furniture to Alexandria. I think this was all a big spiel. I think the British knew exactly what's going on, because when we arrived to the shores, [INAUDIBLE] we saw the cars in Tel Aviv already running. It was middle of the night. We saw the lights of the cars on every-- as we're going sort of onto the shore, big refractors, lights, came all around, and we see three huge British destroyers around us. So they came and sort of told us in a loud speaker that you are in British territorial waters. You follow us. So that means follow us where, I think possibly, they take us into Haifa. And practically all of us were on the board, and all these 2,000-what people, the answer was singing the Hatikva at dark night. It's all the-- this was like a theater, you know, the light showing on us. And-- and just-- really the way we sang, it was-- it was came from heart, not the throat. So there was a few minutes has left where we didn't react, so they started sort of to brush or hit a little bit our-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] our boat. [INAUDIBLE] one hit would have break into half anyhow, so they were throwing these [? letters ?] into the-- you know, to-- whatever, we throw them away.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, some British soldiers came on the-- we just, as they came on the boat, we pushed them down. They were all equipped. They knew it was coming, because they all had this sort of lights and a life jacket. And as the night goes on, it became more and more wild. You know, some people lose judgment.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had very good friend who was extremely protective to me, and very, very strong. He was a Hungarian, was a red head. We call him, even, Piroska. And before these things started, we decided that we're going to lock him down somewhere [? where ?] he could not come out, because we knew that because he was so confident, because of his strength, that he would cause some problem. And on the end, we saw him on the deck, too. Maybe he was banging the door so much that somebody let him out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So, started with these sticks. We're hitting them, throwing them, and the British started to shoot. And I think he was the first victim, because as he had this stick in his hand, he went and-- so the British. The British had a stick, too, and by hitting him back he broke his chin. I mean, anybody normal [INAUDIBLE] would collapse. And he got even much wilder, and he went on to that British soldier, and the British soldier shot him right in the head. So there was three casualties.

speaker: interviewee
text: And in the end we got to Haifa, and so they were waiting for what happens next. So where-- we didn't go into the harbor. It was middle of the harbor, and they put this explosive each time-- you know, something so that should anybody be in the water or something, I mean, they can't reach [? any ?] point. They took us onto-- onto small troop boats-- you know, the fast boats-- and they took us to Cyprus.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who took-- who took you?

speaker: interviewee
text: British Army, or British Navy. And we spent, in Cyprus, 20 months. So basically, I left to go to Israel in 1946, March, and arrived Israel 1949, January. So, it was a long trip. Is there anything else you would like to-- I can go on, and go on, and go on.

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I think there must be a limit--

speaker: interviewer
text: How long did you live in Israel?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Israel, right. Anyhow, when we went to Israel. I mean, we'd been designated to a place to go. Because that was-- we'd all been idealists, Halutzim, and we belonged to the Havurah [INAUDIBLE] movement, which was under the wing of the Mapai party then. And the idea was that once we go to that kibbutz, we're going to get our [INAUDIBLE] on the kibbutz to learn how to establish something, and eventually we will create-- build our own. But by the time-- so many things happened by that kibbutz where we was designated to go. It was during the War of Liberation. It was run over and taken by the Jordanian legion, which was under the command of a [INAUDIBLE] which was [INAUDIBLE]. And it was quite a lot of casualties. And all the rest of them were taken to-- into-- as a prisoner of war. Actually, the first woman prisoner of war in the British history happened there, because the girls were taken as prisoner of war in the prison camp. So, as it turned out, that-- our long term ultimate plan would not eventuate, because they need people to stay there. Because it will replace those who-- who-- and on the end, you know, the whole idea, it-- it did not work really, because you never felt something. You know, in kibbutz everybody's is everybody's. But when you don't create it, it's really not yours. What you create, it's really yours.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was quite an easiness. They were extremely nice to us. But, you know, there is [INAUDIBLE] going, and we started to find if you don't make your own kibbutz we start to find the our own life. So we left, some of us. I did leave, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so in-- in kibbutz, and in Israel together, I've been-- '49-- three years, really. That's exactly three years. And then Frank was in the army so-- still, and when he-- when he came out of the Army, and he went, then I could [INAUDIBLE]-- we wanted to be together in Haifa. And we had sort of a very ideal sort of ideas. We-- we all, I mean, we wanted-- and somehow everything, by circumstances or by another thing, I really don't know, it sort of faded everything. Because you need sort of a definite direction, and being out in Haifa, we-- we established ourselves quite well. But we weren't sort of independent enough to make our own sort of a destiny yet. And when this invitation came from Australia that we should come and visit. Well come-- actually they wanted us to come, but we haven't seen our mother for so many years. And so part of the expenses will be provided in Israel, and part of the expenses were sent from Australia by Alex. Which, we saw that he is a rich man, but the money wasn't his. He had to borrow it. So the story-- I came, and then come back to Australia. I already owe 120 pounds at a time when the wages were, I think, six pounds a week.

speaker: interviewee
text: So if you add all these things together, it was something, you know, which you have to make right. And by the time you pay back your-- your money that you owe, and you-- possibly you save enough money to go back, it's years and years. But I'm glad I'm here, and I really feel well. And-- When did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: Pardon?

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you arrive in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I arrive in Australia In January, 1952.

speaker: interviewer
text: And your mother was living here.

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was here. My mother came to Australia with my eldest brother in 1949.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Edith?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Edith came in 1948, because Edith's husband had some distance relatives in Australia, so they sort of sent them a permit, or anything which we needed. And so it went from-- from-- to Edith, from Edith to Mother, and then-- from Mother it came to us.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so it was through Edith's husband's family that you-- that she came to be here, and then all of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, that's right.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you enjoyed your life in Australia?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I was really-- I think I was 12 months depressed, because I was so much involved, you know, to get to Israel, and being in Israel. Everything was my-- my highest ideas. And you know, it became-- I-- I missed it, I needed it, and I really felt-- and I knew that I wouldn't go back under the circumstances. And really I went back after 20 years or something, yes. But that's-- that was a different Israel and it was a different me.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right, how do you feel that the Holocaust has impacted on your life in total?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's very hard to-- to-- to see. If it's not Holocaust-- I only know one thing. I only knew Holocaust, and I think the Holocaust made me a human being, personally. And the Holocaust made the whole Jewish people real Jewish people, proud Jewish people. The price is too high. It was a terribly high price, but we gained something of value. I don't think-- I can't imagine how else would have ever be what we are now. I mean, today is still anti-Semitism, but today we can stand up against it, be proud. We create beautiful things. I mean, Israel today is a booming, beautiful country. It's-- I mean, we paid a lot for it, and I think the way-- I was-- I am a different human being, now that I went through all these things than I would have been without it. So I let-- let other people judge me. I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. John, can you tell me about the people in the photograph?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I'll start with Father.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: As you can see, he's a lovely man. And he is inside like outside. And the next is Edith. She is his number two child.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mean, she's changed a lot since this picture was taken, but she was always a very pretty girl. And she didn't believe in herself, how-- how much she's loved. She had a bit of a problem with-- with the boys. These older boys are not so loving as they should be, but we-- we did and we do love her very much. And Alex, Alex is the one who-- I think he's the best of our children, all of us. He [? held ?] on his shoulder, everything. He carried the religion, the name, being Jewish, being expressive. Everything which our family is, he take it fully and does it fully.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's wonderful.

speaker: interviewee
text: The other one here is me. It looks a bit out of line. I think I must have turned, or must have did something. And that time people, when they made a photograph, it must have been too costly to repeat it again, so I accept it as me. And Frank, a sweet little kid. He was always so self-assured, they way he sits that even now. And I think from the day he was born he knew what he wants, and he-- he went for it. And thank God. And I'm very proud of him, he achieved it. He is such a strong stalwart for-- for the Judaism, for-- for Gentiles, for everybody. Wherever he can help and, again, promote, he's always there. I think, possibly, that's what my father really wanted to do, if he were to have the means for it. But [INAUDIBLE] so Frank took over the rest. And my mother-- if there is any way I can sort of express, the family loves my father and mother. I can only say beautiful things. Buy my mother is-- this was not only to me, but all the people who knew her, she was a special person--

speaker: interviewer
text: Frank--

speaker: interviewee
text: --and my she rest in peace.

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Frank, ?] more or less, when was that photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think it was in 1935, judging by Frank. I mean, it looks like he's just about going to start school.

speaker: interviewer
text: All right. And where was the photo taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was taken in-- in Lucenec, I think. Yes, it's-- it's the next larger sort of town. I think it was taken in Lucenec.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you. OK, can you explain this photograph to us, [? Frank? ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a custom in Fil'akovo that on Shabbat, or on holidays-- on Yontif-- the men used to go out to the-- to the square, or to the promenade. Or I don't know what you call-- and they sort of discussed topical things. And sort of a normal get together, a friendship, before they went home and they have their lunch. And it was practically in every-- every Saturday. And this was one of the time-- I don't know who, or where, how and why-- somebody was taking this photo. And this represents, really, the bulk of the Jewish men. This is-- women went home and prepared the Shabbos meal, or the lunch, while the men were sort of entertaining themselves. And my father is the tallest one there, which this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: At the back?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, and this is my uncle, who is the eldest uncle. Who is really--

speaker: interviewer
text: Next-- next to him.

speaker: interviewee
text: Next, yes. Now, this-- with this hard hat, or whatever you--

speaker: interviewer
text: The dark hat.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. That was-- it was really the outstanding member of the Lowry family. He was the richer. And-- and the next one is his son, and this is one of their employees. But all sort of people who-- I think if I look at it, there wouldn't many be alive, or maybe none at all now.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, thank you.
